summary: DHH argues that despite paying up to $1,399 for an iPhone, consumers don't truly own their devices because Apple restricts both repairs and software installation. He traces how Apple's control over the App Store represents a startling reversal of computing history, where general-purpose computers always ran whatever software users chose. He draws parallels to historical consumer electronics like VHS players and Walkmans, which never imposed content restrictions. The post concludes that the intuitive principle of 'I bought it, I own it' is being suppressed by Apple's market dominance and safety arguments.
key_points: Apple fights right-to-repair legislation with lobbyists, fake industry groups, and side deals to maintain its repair monopoly
The iPhone's app approval requirement is more restrictive than even Microsoft's anti-competitive behavior against Netscape, which never prevented competitors from running on Windows
All browsers on iPhone are just skins on Safari's engine, giving Apple a hidden chokepoint over web access
If the web were proposed as a new app today, Apple would never approve unfettered, unfiltered access to decentralized content
Historical consumer electronics like VHS players and Walkmans never restricted what content users could consume, making Apple's approach a radical departure
money_quotes: You can spend up to $1,399 on an iPhone 12 Pro Max in the US, but even though the button to commit to this extravagant purchase says "buy", the transaction isn't really a sale in the traditional sense.

Microsoft gave away their browser and preinstalled it. They didn't prevent Netscape from running on Windows. Even that was beyond the pale for one of the most ruthless monopolists in the history of computing at the time.
Imagine if the Sony Walkman could only have played music that Sony approved? And demanded a 30% licensing fee for?
If the web hadn't existed prior to the iPhone, there's no way in hell Apple would ever have approved a browser with unfettered, unfiltered access today. No way.
Application that lets users access any type of content, hosted in a decentralized manner around the world, with no preapproval. DENIED.
There's a completely intuitive and correct line from "I bought it" to "I own it". It's a basic instinctual response, and no amount of market dominance can suppress that forever.
It's a computer. It's my computer. Whether it fits in my pocket or sits on my desk just doesn't matter.
key_insight: Apple's control over iPhone repairs and software installation represents an unprecedented reversal of computing freedom where 'buying' a device no longer means owning it.
